sentences.text: I would like just to go back now, in the little story I just mentioned.
This is before Magnitogorsk.
This is was while I was still in Bobruisk.
I was in Bobruisk.
And I had people starting sending parcels to their relatives in Poland, where people were under the Nazis.
And I organized a parcel.
I even remember knotting the nails together.
I can visualize it.
I remember it.
Send it away to Poland.
And a few months later, I'm getting a letter from my brother-- my father didn't acknowledge it-- from my brother.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SOB] I have to stop now for a minute.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] --thanking me for the parcel, and reminding me that he knows where I am.
And I should remember that there is a God.
There is a God.
Because I knew that I am not religious minded anymore, living among these Bolsheviks.
So he was concerned at that time, while the Nazis were around.
I shouldn't lose my belief.
I was always different to my brother.
Two brothers, two different worlds.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He-- he had an easy death, because I'm sure he, with his belief, went to death, as accept it.
God's will.
Yeah.
Now we can go back there.
Only certain junctions.
Well I can see when I came to Magnitogorsk, and [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] being sent away.
They didn't let us stay there.
We didn't have much trust in the people who were running.
And I was sent away to a place called, and don't ask me to spell it, please, Verkhneural'sk.
This is a Russian word, a place, where there's no train.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think there were gold mines there, far away.
This was the purpose of this [? process. ?] All these people were running there.
Yes.
[SIGHS] It was a very short period.
Because I was with somebody else.
And after a few days, I got up in the morning, and that fellow disappeared.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I said to myself, now is coming winter, because it became late.
And in Ural, it's very, very cold.
And I said to myself, in a time of war, go where it's warm.
Then you'll have less worry.
Because cold, and hunger, is a bad combination.
So I set out back to Magnitogorsk, where I didn't have a very pleasant reception from the authorities.
Running.
Yes.
Just now-- just now you asked about certain events.
I was running, and just no-- I didn't know where to.
I just looked by lights.
You know, just play stranger, strange, far away.
And I was thirsty.
And I heard-- I heard-- the noise of-- what do you call that creature in the mud, that what you have got in the larger size in Queensland?
Frog.
Frog.
Not a toad.
The frogs.
So I went straight to the water.
It was mud.
Stretched myself out, licked the mud.
I asked this outstanding, this is the kind of situation you can remember, of course, because it's--
--so thirsty, and nowhere to go, and nowhere to drink, and nowhere-- you don't know where.
I remember I reached Magnitogorsk.
And I didn't get a very sympathetic reception from the authorities.
Not necessarily.
The confusion was so great, that they tried to control.
But they couldn't.
There were just too many.
Too many.
Too many.
How I succeeded, I don't remember.
I took off by train and went to Middle Asia, a large city called Tashkent.
I reached Tashkent.
I don't know how.
But a large city, this is a million-- a city of millions now.
It's a well-known city now.
And there, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was in a park with thousands and thousands of people, lying in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the park and spending the time, looking for bread, sleeping there.
That was end of summer, '41.
By hook or by crook
Yes.
Now this, I was lucky enough.
While I was lying there in-- in Tashkent in a park, stretched out, with my whole belongings with me--
A shirt perhaps.
I don't know.
Nothing.
I didn't have anything.
The most cherished belong was a piece of bread, a slice of bread.
At that time, it already was a great problem.
Suddenly came an elderly woman, she was from Warsaw, a Jewish woman who was-- her face, I remember.
Her face I definitely remember-- an angelic face.
And she was looking if she has got any relatives that came from Warsaw.
Because there was a flood of people.
And she just approached me and asked my conditions.
And when I explained to her the situation, she said, told me, look.
I live here.
For many years, for many years, since the First World War, when she got married an Armenian Catholic.
I will give you the address.
I leave the door open.
And at nighttime, go in.
And there was a place there where they keep the wood, you know, for heating, and cooking.
You can go in there and sleep there.
And I will leave you a bit of food there as well.
This was-- this was like saving.
It saved me.
That was a great thing.
That is more than winning now the lottery instantly.
I was there for a very long time-- a very long time.
I don't know how long.
Long months.
Many months.
Yes.
Yes, every evening I came with the food there as well.
Never knew.
Yes, I'm sure of that.
Yes.
They are good people.
She was a good person.
Funny thing.
I don't remember her, but I remember his.
He is an Armenian called Sarkis.
This is an Armenian name, Sarkis [?
Kalpavich. ?] His father's name was [?
Karp. ?] Well, his name-- this is-- there's no justification for it.
But I don't remember.
Her face, I remember quite well.
His name, I remember quite well.
My saving angel, yes.
Yes.
Well, what happened then is that after a while, the authorities started making-- I don't how.
There's no word for it even in the English language.
When they close up everything-- [NON-ENGLISH] it's called in Russian-- when they close a whole area and started checking papers, or lack of papers.
There's a word for it in Russian.
Cordoned off the area, yes.
And we were stuck.
And then they send you away where they wanted.
Who was who, and who hadn't the papers necessary.
Well, this is another story.
Somewhere on the way, somebody stole my passport.
Not Polish, by that time-- Russian passport.
My Russian passport, yes.
They didn't have a Polish passport then.
[INAUDIBLE] passport was-- I didn't have a Polish passport in my life.
I was young.
I had a Russian passport, but it was stolen.
In Bobruisk.
Issued in Bobruisk, yes.
This was-- this was a system, a Soviet system, that was a passport with a paragraph.
Which means, have you committed any sin?
Are you a type that is not completely to be trusted?
And I remember the paragraph.
My paragraph was paragraph 38.
That means that I'm coming from Poland, something-- something not kosher.
I'm not to be trusted.
As having-- that's not kosher.
Sorry, sorry an expression.
If you don't follow it, I-- yes.
Not--
--totally to be trusted, yes.
It was stolen.
Well, all I had left in my pocket was a member of the union.
This was, by the way, not voluntary work, the union.
When you work in Russia, you are a member of the union.
And that was on the document also, left in my pocket.
But this-- this document I came to Tashkent.
By the way, when I was in Tashkent and living in that place in--
--in the wood pile, I had to go out in the morning before the gentleman got up, not to see me.
That was very dangerous.
Because I got up in the morning very early.
And the police is everywhere.
Oftentimes I [INAUDIBLE] had problems.
Once I had a problem.
There was such a cordoned-off place.
And I was taken to a place where there's all kind of old things.
And the police were waiting to sort out people, to send them out.
What they did, they sent them out to the villages, called [NON-ENGLISH]. it's called in Russian term-- collective village.
And the trouble was, it wouldn't be bad, because you had what to eat.
But they send you mostly in cotton villages, where they make cotton.
So only thing you could eat was cotton.
Cotton and no food, which was a half death sentence.
Yeah.
I was trapped.
I was trapped to be sent out.
The interesting thing is when-- when they caught you, they didn't ask you the name, because most people didn't have any names.
It was all kinds of vagrants, you know.
So they just give you a number, number 21, 22.
Anyway, I observed some-- a local fellow.
And I had a piece of bread.
I told him, I will give you the piece of bread, and let me in among these junk, to hide.
So after they called, and they call-- after a while it was finished.
And they opened the gates again.
I left.
And this is how I saved myself at that time, from a cotton expedition.
Yes, because there was nothing to eat.
No, no, no job.
That was for a while-- quite a while.
I don't remember how it came to it.
But anyway, after awhile, I shifted to a place outside Tashkent called [?
Lunachowsk ?].
And there I stayed till the end of the war.
I worked there, not work.
I had a working place.
I was a-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Let me think about it.
I remember it in Russian.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] The nearest, it would be a guard.
You know, a guard, a factory.
At night.
And actually, I slept inside.
A watchman.
Oh, thank you.
That was the right word.
That's the right word, yes.
After a factory, yes.
Well, I caught up with them.
They caught up with me.
That was a very convenient place of work.
Because I was not well.
There was very little to eat at that time, very little.
Food situation was horrible, horrible.
Starving.
Sick people, local people, any people.
It was terrible.
You have to realize the men who fell at war.
That's correct.
The collective settlements were empty for-- for men folk.
The situation was terrible.
We got 400 gram of bread a day, and that was all.
Nothing more than that.
And the bread was made of flour and lots of water.
Why lots of water?
Because the people who worked at the factory, in the bakery, stole the flour, and add more water.
So actually the bread was like sticked-- like piece of dough bread.
This we had 400 gram, which was a slice.
And this was for a couple of years, the only source of food.
That was all you could get?
All you could get.
You got a card, yes, and you had coupons.
And the coupons gave you this food.
That was all.
Nothing more.
The official.
Non-official, different story.
Non-officially, you could steal, if you can.
I couldn't.
So you could deal.
I wasn't a great salesman.
I tried, but it was very, very hard.
It was-- these days were terrible.
Luckily, I was so sick, that I was taken to hospital.
The sickness was called [INAUDIBLE].
I think I told you before.
There's a sickness.
People who haven't got the-- don't eat vegetables, sick, skin is cracking up.
This a process that one dies from that, little by little.
But I was taken in time.
Yes.
I was taken to hospital.
Yes.
Yes.
That I was taken to a hospital.
I would say end of '42.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yes. '42.
I'll tell you why.
Because in this place, when Stalingrad was-- the famous Battle of Stalingrad, when the-- the Nazis got their first defeat.
I was in this place.
No.
I was outside of the hospital then.
Yes.
Later.
And you were getting sicker, and sicker, and sicker.
And it was after the fall of Stalingrad, after the victory, the Russian victory and German defeat of Stalingrad?
Yes.
I would like, by the way, now, to add something which I just remembered.
For one period, the source of my income was, I used to come to the post office and write letters for illiterate Russians who wanted to write to their sons on the front.
There were women who couldn't write Russian.
And I used to sit, write for them the letters.
And I used to get an onion, a couple of potatoes, a piece of bread, you know.
[INAUDIBLE] And that was--
Yes.
Yes.
Additional food.
And then I came to hospital.
And in the hospital, I had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the bare minimum.
They didn't feed like here in a hospital.
You got a soup that was made of water and salt and some leaves, and the bread ration, which was the same.
That was quite a long time.
Six months.
Yes.
Approximately six months.
And when I came out of hospital, I found a new vocation.
I started polishing shoes, boots.
My clients were the local people, the Asians who were living on-- on the collective villages, you know.
And these were mostly communist party members.
They used to come for gatherings.
Yes.
And it was a dusty place.
They used to come.
A shoe polishing outfit.
And they used to call me professor, because I had glasses.
That was a sign.
The shoe polishing professor.
And this was already-- this time started already to get easier.
Because with-- with the time-- as a matter of fact, by the beginning of 1944, the situation got easier with food.
Increased, so the food situation was-- why, I don't know.
But--
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Weak, but healthy.
Yes.
I came out and started working in this shoe business, which was.
Yes.
This was additional.
The watchman was at night, and this was the day.
Yes.
And night job, this I-- this I kept till I-- till I left Russia on the way back to Poland, by the end of the war.
Yes.
I slept there at night.
Yes.
A place on the floor.
Yes.
Two jobs, and I don't remember being hungry anymore.
Because the bad times during the worst time of the war, the constant nagging thought was, where do I get something to eat?
Yes.
Yes.
Well at that time I wasn't interested in not listening to their propaganda.
Their propaganda was my hope for surviving.
I used to come in the middle of the day in the library.
And I used to take the newspapers.
Yes.
Yeah, not at Tashkent's, [?
Lunachowsk's. ?] This is--
And I used to read.
Besides, in the street were loudspeakers.
Yes?
And they used to give the news, what happens, what the situation is in the world.
So I had an idea.
I remember by the way, the first time-- that was the end of the war, of course.
That was in August '45.
I was sitting here reading the paper, that Americans dropped a bomb that was 20,000 ton, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the force of 20,000 ton of TNT.
And I said to myself, this must be a mistake.
This is impossible.
A bomb is 5,000.
That was the first atom bomb.
Yes.
Yes.
Oh the newspapers was the newspapers.
The Russian newspapers all [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] propaganda, propaganda, and propaganda.
And if you got a little bit of brain, you have just to understand and decide for yourself what is true and what is not true.
But mostly, the situation, the front you could visualize.
Let's say if when the Germans were going forward and conquering this city, and that city, you used to say, after a long and bitter struggle, the city was evacuated.
This was the language.
So I knew what had happened.

And let's say when Stalingrad, which was the turning point, Stalingrad, that was, let's see, January '43 was the turning point in the Second World War, where the Germans-- I at that time, made up my mind, I, myself-- the Germans have lost the war.
It was the first time that the whole army was surrounded, German army defeated.
And as a matter of fact, from that time, the Russians started going forward all the time.
That was the last piece of land that the Germans reached, was this.
From then on--
It was back the other way.
Back the other way.
No.
I somehow had a hope I would survive.
I don't-- I couldn't visualize otherwise.
Otherwise I wouldn't have survived, perhaps.
Yes.
Oh yes.
Yes.
From 1943, they-- they said they have taken 110, 120,000 Germans-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] how do you say?
And also I remember the Russian word, [INAUDIBLE].
They have taken them in [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] captivity.
Would that be correct word?
With the field marshal.
This was the beginning of the end of the Germans, of the Nazi Germany.
As far as I remember, the uprising in Warsaw ghetto, I remember in '43.
Yes.
No.
They usually-- they usually tried to hide the Jewish part of it, for obvious reason.
The Nazis presented their struggle-- their war as a war against Jews.
The Russians, the communists, presented their struggle as a struggle for freedom, for democracy.
Now if you say the word, Jews, you-- you bring in your work, the Jewish term again.
So they very seldom used the word Jews.
Very seldom.
That was, yes, that was as an event.
Until September or October '45.
At that stage, I had already-- people started disappearing.
What means disappearing?
People, together with us, the Polish colony, started little, one by one, disappearing.
So we got to know that they are leaving.
What I did is I looked up again this woman.
The lady.
And I asked her if she can get for me documents, I should be able to get out and go back to Poland.
And this is how it happened.
Yes.
Yes.
For me and a group of three people, four people who left.
No.
Documents for each one of us.
Four people.
Four lots of documents, yes.
Forgeries, yes.
Obviously forgeries.
That we are officers going back from-- from a special job.
This was a highly risky-- looking back, you see, I took a great risk.
What happened?
[INAUDIBLE] What happened?
I went from there to Moscow, via Kiev.
No.
To Moscow.
First to Moscow.
This is a little story in itself.
I went to Moscow, from Moscow to Kiev.
And from Kiev, I was in Moscow a few days.
And from Kiev, I went by train to-- these documents had the destination, Lviv.
Lviv was the Soviet side.
Krakow was already not the Soviet side.
On the train, while we were travelling, the four of us, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was such an institution, Soviet Russia's train police, police who just bother about the trains.
They cordoned off the train and started looking for documents.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And these four people of us, each one of us looked so typical Jewish, non-military type, that we had decided to divide our group of four, two and two.
And so not to-- the attention shouldn't be so obvious.
Anyway, so the police came up, a police officer.
Secret police, [NON-ENGLISH] it was called at that time.
[INAUDIBLE].
Yes, this is abbreviation.
A fellow took the documents, started looking.
And I nearly lost my soul.
Because I knew it could be end of my life.
He looked at me, and he said in Russian-- he said in Russian-- you're going to Lviv, and Lviv to Krakow?
Which means the other side.
You want to over the border.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I said, yes.
He shook my hands, and just gave a sign that he's Jewish.
Yes.
Otherwise, after train, to jail, and the end.
So you had to be lucky quite a few times.
Not once.
Yes.
